content.full_text:
County of San Luis Obispo

County Government Center • San Luis Obispo, California 93408
Room 306 • Phone 549-5465

Office of
Deferred Compensation
and
Defined Contribution
Plan & Trust

August 30, 1990


Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807
ATTN: Legal Department

RE: Filing Requirements
Form 721

Greetings:

After a telephone conversation with Ms. Peggy
Bernardi of your office on August 29, 1990, I am writing
this letter for a formal opinion on the requirement or
obligation to file Form 721, Statement of Economic
Interests.

My situation is as follows: I am staffing a
temporary deferred compensation advisory committee. This
committee will be made up of five to nine county employees
whose knowledge, background and interest are relevant to
lending sound analysis and advice to the deferred
compensation plan administrator. These individuals are
invited by the Plan Administrator to serve as a committee
member. The sole objective of this committee is to solicit
Request for Proposals to prospective investment providers
for the County's deferred compensation assets. They will
then review all submitted proposals and make a
recommendation to the plan administrator on a selection of
one or more investment providers for deferred compensation
assets. The Plan Administrator will take these
recommendation into consideration, however he will take his
own recommendation (which will probably be the
recommendation submitted by the committee) to the Board of
Supervisors for adoption. Once a recommendation has been
made to the Plan Administrator, the duties of the committee
have been concluded and the committee will be disbanded.

Page 2                                                  FPPC


     As I am sure you are aware, Internal Revenue Code 457
deferred compensation assets are considered assets of the
employer (the County) until they are distributed to the
employee. The Plan Administrator of the deferred compensation
program is also the County Treasurer - who is
required to file a FORM 721. The question lies in whether
the committee is also required or obligated to file a FORM
721 because they are rendering advice to the Plan
Administrator.

     The language in the preface to the instruction manual
for FORM 721 says that:
     "...All other public officials and employees who
     manage public investments including but not limited
     to:
          Employees and consultants who have decision
          making authority with regard to investment of
          city, county or state funds. This includes,
          but is not limited to, pension fund managers
          for public agencies, as well as any person to
          whom a city or county treasurer has delegated
          such authority..." (emphasis added)

     I directed this question to our County Counsel and
the response was that these committee members would
"probably be required" to file a FORM 721. This answer is
based on a very broad interpretation of the present
information available as stated above. Counsel opines that
since the committee is making a recommendation regarding an
investment provider, they indirectly have decision making
authority by virtue of the delegation to review and make a
recommendation on who would handle the investment of county
funds.

     This is the second appointment of a deferred
compensation advisory committee. The first committee
appointment in the fall of 1987. The recommendations from
the first committee were accepted by the Plan
Administrator.

     Specifically, my questions are:

1.   Are the temporary deferred compensation advisory
     committee members required to file a Form 721?

2.   If yes, are they required to file both "entering
     office" and a "leaving office" forms at the beginning and
     conclusion of their committee duties (which could last for
     up to 6 months at the most).

Page 3                                                    FPPC


3.    If no, are they required to file another conflict of
interest or statement of economic interest form now?

4.    If no, will future committee members be required to
file a Form 721 or other form in the future (taking into
consideration that every three to five years a committee
will probably be appointed to evaluate potential investment
providers for deferred compensation assets.

     During our phone conversation, Ms. Bernardi
seemed to think that because this committee has only
performed on one other occasion, we have not yet set a
precedent as to being required to file a Form 721.
Further, because the committee only provides a
recommendation to the Plan Administrator, and it is not
binding, that the committee members have not been afforded
the authority to make decisions regarding the investment of
public funds.

     I look forward to your prompt response to the above
questions. If you have any questions, you may reach me at
805-549-5465, in the office of Retirement Plans.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]
                                        Andrea Paley
                                        Pension Analyst
                                        Staff to Deferred
                                        Compensation Committee


cc: Frank L. Freitas
    Plan Administrator

sections.question:
s. If you have any guestions, you may reach me at
805-549-5465, in the office of Retirement Plans.
